attributes.stem:
A 45-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic with a six-month history of episodic shortness of breath and chest tightness. These episodes of breathlessness are worse with exposure to cold air and during the winter season. Four years ago, he worked in a chemical factory and experienced several similar episodes at that time. Notably, his symptoms improved during weekends and holidays when away from work, but returned upon resuming his duties at the factory. He left this job four years ago due to worsening respiratory symptoms. He has a 20 pack-year smoking history and does not take any regular medications.

On examination, he appears mildly distressed. His pulse rate is 100/min, blood pressure 120/80 mmHg, respiratory rate 22/min, SpO2 96% on room air, and temperature 36.8°C. On auscultation, bilateral expiratory wheezes are heard over both lung fields.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following investigations is most useful in confirming the diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with episodic respiratory symptoms including shortness of breath, chest tightness, and wheezing that are characteristic of asthma. The history of symptoms worsening with cold air exposure and and clear occupational triggers (chemical factory work with improvement away from workplace) further supports this diagnosis. This pattern strongly suggests occupational asthma, which can persist even after cessation of exposure to the causative agent.

To confirm asthma, the most appropriate initial investigation is spirometry with bronchodilator reversibility testing. Spirometry can demonstrate airflow obstruction (reduced FEV1/FVC ratio) and, importantly, reversibility of this obstruction after bronchodilator administration, which is a hallmark of asthma.

A chest X-ray is often normal in asthma and has limited diagnostic value for confirming the condition. It may be useful to exclude alternative diagnoses such as pneumonia, pneumothorax, or structural lung abnormalities, but it cannot confirm asthma. In this patient with a significant smoking history, a chest X-ray might be considered to rule out other smoking-related lung diseases, but it would not be the most useful test for confirming asthma.

Bronchoscopy with washings is an invasive procedure that allows direct visualisation of the airways and collection of samples for cytological and microbiological analysis. It is not indicated as a first-line investigation for suspected asthma. Even with the history of chemical exposure, bronchoscopy would not be expected to reveal any findings that would assist with diagnosing asthma. The changes in asthma are primarily functional rather than structural or cytological.

Peak flow monitoring over time can be useful in asthma management and can help demonstrate variability in airflow limitation, which is characteristic of asthma. While serial peak flow measurements showing diurnal variation of >10% support an asthma diagnosis, spirometry provides more comprehensive information about lung function and is the preferred initial diagnostic test. In cases of suspected occupational asthma, serial peak flow monitoring at work and away from work may also be valuable, but spirometry remains the gold standard for initial diagnosis.

Allergy testing (skin prick tests or specific IgE) can identify sensitisation to environmental allergens that may trigger asthma symptoms in some patients. However, a positive allergy test alone does not confirm asthma, and many patients with asthma do not have identifiable allergic triggers. In this case, with occupational and cold air triggers, allergy testing would not be the most useful initial investigation.

Two main types of occupational asthma exist: allergic occupational asthma (immunologically mediated) and irritant-induced occupational asthma (direct airway damage from high-level exposures). Common causative agents include: isocyanates (polyurethane, paints), flour and grain dust (bakers, millers), latex (healthcare workers), cleaning agents, wood dust, and metalworking fluids. Management includes: immediate removal from exposure, standard asthma treatment, and consideration of workplace modifications or job redeployment.
learningPoints: Asthma causes wheezing, breathlessness, chest tightness, and coughing that varies over time and in intensity.
Spirometry with bronchodilator reversibility testing is the most useful investigation for confirming a diagnosis of asthma.
Occupational asthma should be considered in patients with respiratory symptoms that improve away from the workplace.
references: Hashmi MF, Cataletto ME. Asthma. 2024 May 3. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan–. PMID: 28613651. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28613651/

Lamb K, Theodore D, Bhutta BS. Spirometry. [Updated 2023 Aug 17]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK560526/

Celli BR. The importance of spirometry in COPD and asthma: effect on approach to management. Chest. 2000 Feb;117(2 Suppl):15S-9S. doi: 10.1378/chest.117.2_suppl.15s. PMID: 10673468. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/10673468/
